content:
# Exodus Chapter 22 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~45r-45v (PDF pp. 88-89); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 64-65 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_022_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_022_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- CONTINUATION OF THE BOOK OF THE COVENANT:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 22 in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This chapter continues the casuistic legal corpus of the Book of the Covenant (*Sefer ha-Berit*), moving from the laws of personal injury and liability (ch. 21) into laws governing theft, property damage, and deposit (vv. 1-15), then transitioning into a series of apodictic moral and social commands concerning seduction, sorcery, bestiality, idolatry, treatment of the vulnerable, lending, and consecration (vv. 16-31). A critical versification difference must be noted: the MT's 22:1 corresponds to the LXX's 21:37, and the MT's 22:2-31 corresponds to the LXX's 22:1-30. This one-verse offset persists throughout the chapter. The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 64-65) presents the LXX rendering in continuous uncial script across three columns per page.

---

## Laws Concerning Theft and Restitution (vv. 1-4)

**22:1** If a man steals an ox or a sheep and slaughters it or sells it, five cattle he shall pay for the ox, and four of the flock for the sheep. [fn1]

**22:2** If in the breaking-in the thief is found and he is struck and dies, there is no bloodguilt for him. [fn2]

**22:3** If the sun has risen upon him, there is bloodguilt for him. He shall surely pay; if he has nothing, then he shall be sold for his theft. [fn3]

**22:4** If the stolen thing is surely found in his hand alive -- whether ox or donkey or sheep -- double he shall pay. [fn4]

---

## Laws Concerning Crop and Field Damage (vv. 5-6)

**22:5** If a man causes a field or a vineyard to be grazed over and lets loose his livestock and it grazes in the field of another, the best of his field and the best of his vineyard he shall pay. [fn5]

**22:6** If fire goes out and finds thorns, and a stack of grain or the standing grain or the field is consumed, the one who kindled the burning shall surely pay. [fn6]

---

## Laws Concerning Deposits and Trust (vv. 7-13)

**22:7** If a man gives to his neighbor silver or goods to keep and it is stolen from the house of the man -- if the thief is found, he shall pay double. [fn7]

**22:8** If the thief is not found, then the owner of the house shall be brought near to ha-Elohim, to determine whether he did not lay his hand upon the property of his neighbor. [fn8]

**22:9** For every matter of transgression -- for an ox, for a donkey, for a sheep, for a garment, for any lost thing of which one says, "This is it" -- the case of both of them shall come before ha-Elohim; he whom Elohim declares guilty shall pay double to his neighbor. [fn9]

**22:10** If a man gives to his neighbor a donkey or an ox or a sheep or any animal to keep, and it dies or is injured or is driven away, no one seeing -- [fn10]

**22:11** an oath of YHWH shall be between them both, whether he did not lay his hand upon the property of his neighbor; and its owner shall accept it, and he shall not pay. [fn11]

**22:12** And if it is indeed stolen from him, he shall pay to its owner. [fn12]

**22:13** If it is indeed torn by a beast, let him bring it as evidence; the torn thing he shall not pay. [fn13]

---

## Laws Concerning Borrowed Property (vv. 14-15)

**22:14** And if a man borrows from his neighbor and it is injured or dies, its owner not being with it, he shall surely pay. [fn14]

**22:15** If its owner is with it, he shall not pay. If it is hired, it comes in its hire. [fn15]

---

## Laws Concerning Seduction (vv. 16-17)

**22:16** And if a man seduces a virgin who is not betrothed and lies with her, he shall surely pay a bride-price for her as a wife. [fn16]

**22:17** If her father utterly refuses to give her to him, silver he shall weigh out according to the bride-price of virgins. [fn17]

---

## Apodictic Laws: Capital Offenses and Exclusive Worship (vv. 18-20)

**22:18** A sorceress you shall not let live. [fn18]

**22:19** Anyone who lies with a beast shall surely be put to death. [fn19]

**22:20** The one who sacrifices to gods shall be devoted to destruction -- except to YHWH alone. [fn20]

---

## Laws Protecting the Vulnerable (vv. 21-24)

**22:21** And a sojourner you shall not wrong and you shall not oppress him, for sojourners you were in the land of Egypt. [fn21]

**22:22** Any widow or orphan you shall not afflict. [fn22]

**22:23** If you surely afflict him, and if he surely cries out to me, I will surely hear his cry. [fn23]

**22:24** And my anger shall burn, and I will kill you with the sword, and your wives shall become widows and your children orphans. [fn24]

---

## Laws Concerning Lending and Pledges (vv. 25-27)

**22:25** If you lend silver to my people, to the poor who is with you, you shall not be to him as a creditor; you shall not lay upon him interest. [fn25]

**22:26** If you indeed take as pledge the garment of your neighbor, before the sun goes down you shall return it to him. [fn26]

**22:27** For it is his only covering; it is his garment for his skin. In what shall he lie down? And it shall be, when he cries out to me, that I will hear, for gracious am I. [fn27]

---

## Laws Concerning Reverence and Firstfruits (vv. 28-31)

**22:28** God you shall not revile, and a leader among your people you shall not curse. [fn28]

**22:29** Your fullness and your outflow you shall not delay. The firstborn of your sons you shall give to me. [fn29]

**22:30** So you shall do with your ox and with your flock: seven days it shall be with its mother; on the eighth day you shall give it to me. [fn30]

**22:31** And men of holiness you shall be to me. And flesh in the field torn by beasts you shall not eat; to the dog you shall cast it. [fn31]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Ki yignov ish shor o-seh u-tevakho o mekharo khamishah vaqar yeshallem takhat ha-shor ve-arba tson takhat ha-seh*: "If a man steals an ox or a sheep and slaughters it or sells it, five cattle he shall pay for the ox, and four of the flock for the sheep"** -- EMET renders "five cattle he shall pay for the ox, and four of the flock for the sheep." LOGOS renders "five oxen he shall pay for the ox, and four sheep for the sheep." PNEUMA renders "the thief must pay back five head of cattle for the ox and four sheep for the one sheep." The consensus follows EMET for the Hebrew terms *baqar* ("cattle") and *tson* ("flock") rather than LOGOS's repetition of "oxen/sheep" or PNEUMA's "head of cattle." The Hebrew deliberately uses the collective nouns *baqar* and *tson* rather than repeating *shor* and *seh*. The five-to-four asymmetry reflects the ox's greater value as a draft animal. All three translators agree on the basic structure.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Im ba-makhteret yimmatse ha-gannav ve-hukkah va-met ein lo damim*: "If in the breaking-in the thief is found and he is struck and dies, there is no bloodguilt for him"** -- EMET renders "if in the breaking-in the thief is found." LOGOS renders "if the thief is found breaking in." PNEUMA renders "if a thief is caught breaking in." The consensus follows EMET's word order, which preserves the Hebrew syntax placing *ba-makhteret* ("in the breaking-in") first, establishing the nocturnal scenario before introducing the thief. LOGOS and PNEUMA reorder for English readability but lose the Hebrew emphasis. The term *makhteret* (from *khatar*, "to dig through") refers to tunneling through a mud-brick wall. The legal principle: nighttime self-defense incurs no bloodguilt because the homeowner cannot assess the intruder's intent.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Shallem yeshallem*: "he shall surely pay" vs. "he shall surely make restitution" vs. "he must make full restitution"** -- EMET renders "he shall surely pay." LOGOS renders "he shall surely make restitution." PNEUMA renders "the thief must make full restitution." The consensus follows EMET's "he shall surely pay" -- preserving the infinitive absolute doubling ("surely") per binding convention. While LOGOS's "make restitution" captures the restorative sense of the piel of *shalam*, the chapter uses *yeshallem* so frequently (vv. 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15) that a shorter rendering is preferable. "Pay" is used consistently throughout this chapter to render *yeshallem*, with the understanding that payment is restorative, not merely transactional. PNEUMA drops the infinitive absolute construction.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Himmatse timmatse*: infinitive absolute rendering** -- EMET renders "if the stolen thing is surely found." LOGOS renders "if the stolen animal is found." PNEUMA renders "if the stolen animal is actually found." The consensus follows EMET, preserving the infinitive absolute doubling ("surely found") per binding convention. LOGOS drops the doubling entirely. PNEUMA's "actually" is an informal equivalent. The Hebrew *himmatse timmatse* (niphal infinitive absolute + imperfect) emphasizes the certainty of discovery. The term *ha-genevah* ("the stolen thing") is rendered generically rather than as "animal" (LOGOS/PNEUMA), since the Hebrew does not specify -- though context implies livestock.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Yav'er*: "causes to be grazed" vs. "causes a field to be grazed over" vs. "lets livestock graze"** -- EMET renders "causes to be grazed a field." LOGOS renders "causes a field or vineyard to be grazed over." PNEUMA renders "lets livestock graze in a field." The consensus follows LOGOS for readability while preserving the hiphil force of *yav'er* ("causes to be grazed"). PNEUMA restructures the sentence and loses the Hebrew causative. The root *ba'ar* ("to graze/consume") in v. 5 creates a wordplay with the same root meaning "to burn" in v. 6 -- a paronomasia impossible to capture in English.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Ham-mav'ir et-ha-be'erah*: "the one who kindled the burning"** -- All three translators agree on substance. EMET renders "the one who kindled the burning." LOGOS renders "the one who kindled the fire." PNEUMA renders "the one who started the fire." The consensus follows EMET -- "the one who kindled the burning" -- preserving the Hebrew *be'erah* ("burning"), which is cognate with the root *ba'ar* used in v. 5 for grazing. LOGOS and PNEUMA lose this connection by substituting "fire."

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Kelim*: "vessels" vs. "goods" vs. "goods"** -- EMET renders "vessels." LOGOS renders "goods." PNEUMA renders "goods." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA -- "goods" -- as the more functional English rendering of *kelim*, which encompasses any crafted articles of value (not only "vessels" in the modern English sense of containers).

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Ha-Elohim*: "God," "the judges," or retained** -- EMET renders "to God [ha-Elohim]." LOGOS renders "to God." PNEUMA renders "before God." The consensus retains "ha-Elohim" untranslated, consistent with the precedent established in 21:6. The term *ha-Elohim* in a juridical context may designate God directly (a sacral oath at the sanctuary), divinely appointed judges, or both simultaneously. The niphal *ve-niqrav* ("he shall be brought near") suggests a cultic act of presentation. The LXX renders *enopion tou theou* ("before God"), favoring the divine reading. The plural verb *yarshi'un* in v. 9 adds further complexity: it may support the "judges" interpretation (true plural subject) or be an honorific plural with a singular divine referent. Preserving "ha-Elohim" allows the reader to encounter both resonances.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Devar* and *avedah*: "matter/case" and "lost thing"** -- EMET renders "matter of transgression...lost thing." LOGOS renders "matter of transgression...lost thing." PNEUMA renders "case of disputed ownership...lost item." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "matter of transgression...lost thing" -- preserving the Hebrew *pesha* ("transgression, breach of trust") and *avedah* ("lost thing"). PNEUMA's "disputed ownership" is interpretive; the Hebrew specifically names the category as *pesha* (willful breach), not mere dispute. PNEUMA's "lost item" is acceptable but "lost thing" is closer to the Hebrew abstraction.

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Nishbar...nishbah*: "is injured...is driven away"** -- EMET renders "is injured or is driven away." LOGOS renders "is injured or is driven away." PNEUMA renders "is injured, or is driven off." All three agree on substance. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. The Hebrew *nishbar* (niphal of *shavar*, "to break") literally means "is broken" but "injured" captures the sense for English readers. The Hebrew *nishbah* (niphal of *shavah*, "to take captive") = "is carried off" by raiders or wild animals.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Shevu'at YHWH*: "an oath of YHWH"** -- All three translators agree: "an oath of YHWH" (EMET), "an oath of YHWH" (LOGOS), "an oath before YHWH" (PNEUMA). The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "an oath of YHWH" -- preserving the construct chain. PNEUMA's "before" is interpretive; the Hebrew says *shevu'at YHWH* (genitive), not a locative construction. This is the only occurrence in the Torah of a sworn oath designated by the divine name in a property dispute. The oath invokes YHWH as witness and enforcer when no human witness exists (*ein ro'eh*, v. 10). The LXX renders *horkos tou theou* ("oath of God"), substituting *theos* for the Tetragrammaton per standard Septuagintal practice.

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Ganov yigganev*: infinitive absolute** -- EMET renders "if it is surely stolen." LOGOS renders "if it is indeed stolen." PNEUMA renders "if the animal was actually stolen." The consensus follows EMET -- "if it is indeed stolen" (adopting LOGOS's "indeed" as a stylistic variant of "surely" to avoid monotony). The infinitive absolute *ganov yigganev* (qal infinitive absolute + niphal imperfect) emphasizes the reality of the theft. PNEUMA drops the emphatic construction.

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Tarof yittaref*: infinitive absolute; *ed*: "witness" vs. "evidence"** -- EMET renders "if it is surely torn, let him bring it as witness; the torn he shall not pay." LOGOS renders "if it is indeed torn by a beast, let him bring it as evidence; he shall not make restitution for that which was torn." PNEUMA renders "if it was torn apart by a wild beast, the keeper shall bring the remains as evidence." The consensus follows a blend: "if it is indeed torn by a beast, let him bring it as evidence; the torn thing he shall not pay." The infinitive absolute is preserved. LOGOS's "evidence" is preferred over EMET's literal "witness" for *ed* in this context -- the physical remains serve as *evidence*, not as a human *witness*. EMET's "the torn" is adjusted to "the torn thing" for clarity. PNEUMA restructures the syntax entirely.

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *Shallem yeshallem*: infinitive absolute** -- All three preserve the emphatic construction: EMET "he shall surely pay," LOGOS "he shall surely make restitution," PNEUMA "full restitution must be made." The consensus follows EMET for consistency with v. 3: "he shall surely pay."

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Ba bi-sekharo*: "it comes in its hire"** -- EMET renders "it comes in its hire." LOGOS renders "it came for its hire." PNEUMA renders "the loss is covered by the hiring fee." The consensus follows EMET -- "it comes in its hire" -- preserving the Hebrew's terse, somewhat enigmatic formula. LOGOS changes the tense. PNEUMA paraphrases entirely. The Hebrew *ba bi-sekharo* literally means "it enters by means of its hire" -- the risk of loss was factored into the rental price.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Mahor yimharenah*: "he shall surely pay a bride-price"** -- EMET renders "he shall surely pay a bride-price for her as a wife." LOGOS renders "he shall surely pay the bride-price for her to be his wife." PNEUMA renders "he must pay the bride-price in full and take her as his wife." The consensus follows EMET. The infinitive absolute *mahor yimharenah* ("paying-bride-price he shall pay-bride-price for her") is emphatic. PNEUMA's "in full" is interpretive and restructures the sentence; it also drops the emphatic construction.

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Ma'en yema'en*: "utterly refuses"** -- All three translators agree: "utterly refuses." The consensus preserves the infinitive absolute doubling. EMET and LOGOS place the father as subject after the verbal phrase; PNEUMA restructures. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS word order.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Mekhashefah*: "sorceress" and gender** -- EMET renders "a sorceress you shall not let live." LOGOS renders "you shall not allow a sorceress to live." PNEUMA renders "you must not allow a sorceress to live." The consensus follows EMET's word order -- "a sorceress you shall not let live" -- preserving the Hebrew's fronted object (*mekhashefah*), which places emphasis on the category. The feminine form *mekhashefah* (piel participle feminine of *kashaf*) is preserved; though the prohibition extends to both sexes (cf. Deut 18:10), the Hebrew uses the feminine specifically. The LXX broadens to masculine plural *pharmakous* ("sorcerers/poisoners"). The consensus translates what the Hebrew says, not what it may imply.

> **[fn19] v.19 -- *Mot yumat*: "shall surely be put to death"** -- EMET renders "shall be surely put to death." LOGOS renders "shall surely be put to death." PNEUMA renders "must be put to death." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the infinitive absolute doubling. PNEUMA's "must" drops the emphatic construction.

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *Yokhoram*: "shall be devoted to destruction" vs. "shall be utterly destroyed"** -- EMET renders "shall be devoted to destruction." LOGOS renders "shall be utterly destroyed." PNEUMA renders "must be devoted to destruction." The consensus follows EMET/PNEUMA -- "shall be devoted to destruction" -- because the hophal of *kharam* specifically denotes sacral destruction (*kherem*), not ordinary execution. LOGOS's "utterly destroyed" captures the finality but loses the sacral-dedicatory dimension. The *kherem* places the offender in the category of that which is wholly given over to YHWH through annihilation. The qualifying *bilti la-YHWH le-vaddo* ("except to YHWH alone") is the exclusive claim of the first commandment expressed in legal consequence.

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Ger*: "sojourner" vs. "stranger"** -- EMET renders "a sojourner." LOGOS renders "a sojourner." PNEUMA renders "a stranger." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "sojourner" -- as the established technical term for *ger* in biblical scholarship. PNEUMA's "stranger" loses the specificity of the *ger* as a resident alien (as distinct from *nokhri*, "foreigner," and *toshav*, "temporary settler"). The motivation clause -- "for sojourners you were in the land of Egypt" -- grounds the ethic in Israel's own experience of alienation.

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *Almanah ve-yatom*: "widow or orphan" vs. "widow or fatherless child"** -- EMET renders "any widow or orphan." LOGOS renders "any widow or orphan." PNEUMA renders "any widow or fatherless child." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "widow or orphan." PNEUMA's "fatherless child" is an accurate gloss of *yatom* but unnecessary; "orphan" is the standard English rendering and well understood.

> **[fn23] v.23 -- Triple infinitive absolute: *anneh te'anneh...tsa'oq yits'aq...shamo'a eshma*: the chain of emphatic verbs** -- EMET renders "if you surely afflict him and if he surely cries out to me, I will surely hear his cry." LOGOS renders "if you indeed afflict him -- for if he cries out to me, I will surely hear his cry." PNEUMA renders "if you do take advantage of them and they cry out to me, I will certainly hear their cry." The consensus follows EMET, preserving all three infinitive absolute constructions with "surely" per binding convention. The triple emphatic chain -- your certain affliction triggers his certain cry triggers my certain hearing -- is one of the most rhetorically powerful constructions in the Torah. LOGOS preserves only two of three doublings. PNEUMA drops all three. The shift to the divine first person ("to me...I will hear") marks the transition from judicial ruling to divine threat.

> **[fn24] v.24 -- *Ve-kharah appi*: "my anger shall burn" vs. "my anger will burn"** -- All three translators agree on substance. EMET renders "and my anger shall burn." LOGOS renders "then my anger shall burn." PNEUMA renders "my anger will burn." The consensus follows EMET, preserving the Hebrew conjunction "And." The measure-for-measure justice -- oppressors' wives become widows, their children become orphans -- is the most severe divine threat in the Book of the Covenant.

> **[fn25] v.25 -- *Et-ammi et-he-ani*: "my people, the poor"** -- EMET renders "my people, the poor who is with you." LOGOS renders "my people, to the poor who is with you." PNEUMA renders "any of my people -- to the poor among you." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. The double *et* construction (*et-ammi et-he-ani*) is appositional: "my people" = "the poor who is with you." The term *neshekh* ("interest," literally "bite," from *nashakh*, "to bite") is a vivid metaphor: interest devours the borrower's substance.

> **[fn26] v.26 -- *Khavol takhbol*: infinitive absolute** -- EMET renders "if you indeed take as pledge." LOGOS renders "if you indeed take as pledge." PNEUMA renders "if you take your neighbor's cloak as collateral." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the infinitive absolute. PNEUMA drops the emphatic construction and substitutes the modern financial term "collateral" for "pledge."

> **[fn27] v.27 -- *Khannun ani*: "gracious am I" vs. "I am gracious" vs. "I am compassionate"** -- EMET renders "gracious am I." LOGOS renders "I am gracious." PNEUMA renders "I am compassionate." The consensus follows EMET -- "gracious am I" -- preserving the Hebrew predicate-first word order (*khannun ani*), which places emphasis on the divine attribute. LOGOS normalizes to English subject-first order. PNEUMA substitutes "compassionate" for "gracious" -- the Hebrew *khannun* (from *khanan*, "to show favor, be gracious") is one of YHWH's core self-descriptions that will be expanded in the great theophanic formula of 34:6. "Gracious" is the standard English rendering. The LXX's *eleemon* ("merciful") represents a different semantic choice. The rhetorical question "In what shall he lie down?" pierces the legal abstraction with human pathos.

> **[fn28] v.28 -- *Elohim*: "God" vs. "God" (without article); *nasi*: "leader" vs. "ruler" vs. "leader"** -- EMET renders "God you shall not revile, and a leader among your people you shall not curse." LOGOS renders "God you shall not revile, and a ruler among your people you shall not curse." PNEUMA renders "you must not revile God, and you must not curse a leader of your people." The consensus follows EMET for *nasi* as "leader" (a more neutral rendering than LOGOS's "ruler," which may imply monarchical authority absent from the pre-monarchic context). Note that *Elohim* here appears without the article (unlike vv. 8-9), so the consensus renders it as "God" rather than retaining "ha-Elohim." The LXX renders the plural *theous* ("gods"), which may reflect a reading of *Elohim* as a true plural referring to judges or dignitaries. The consensus follows the singular divine reading, which is the majority tradition. PNEUMA reverses the Hebrew word order (object-verb to subject-verb).

> **[fn29] v.29 -- *Mele'atekha ve-dim'akha*: "your fullness and your outflow"** -- EMET renders "your fullness and your outflow." LOGOS renders "your fullness and your overflow." PNEUMA renders "the fullness of your harvest or the overflow of your presses." The consensus follows EMET -- "your fullness and your outflow" -- preserving the Hebrew's compressed, metonymic language. *Mele'ah* ("fullness") denotes the ripe grain harvest; *dim'ah* ("outflow, tears, dripping") denotes the juice from pressed grapes or olives. The LXX makes the referents explicit: *aparchas alonos kai lenou* ("firstfruits of threshing floor and wine-vat"). The Hebrew's brevity is deliberate and should not be expanded. PNEUMA adds interpretive glosses ("of your harvest," "of your presses") not in the Hebrew.

> **[fn30] v.30 -- *Ken ta'aseh*: "so you shall do" vs. "likewise you shall do"** -- EMET renders "so you shall do." LOGOS renders "likewise you shall do." PNEUMA renders "you must do the same." The consensus follows EMET -- "so" for *ken*. LOGOS's "likewise" is acceptable but slightly more formal than needed. PNEUMA restructures and drops the conjunction.

> **[fn31] v.31 -- *Ve-anshei-qodesh tihyun li*: "men of holiness you shall be to me"** -- EMET renders "and men of holiness you shall be to me." LOGOS renders "and men of holiness you shall be to me." PNEUMA renders "you are to be people set apart for me." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "and men of holiness you shall be to me" -- preserving the Hebrew construct chain *anshei-qodesh* ("men of holiness"), which makes holiness the governing noun. This is distinct from "holy men" (adjective + noun); the Hebrew syntax subordinates the people to holiness, not holiness to the people. PNEUMA's "people set apart" is a dynamic paraphrase that loses both the construct chain and the specific term *qodesh* ("holiness"). The LXX renders *andres hagioi* ("holy men"), reversing the Hebrew construct chain into a simple adjective-noun phrase. The dietary prohibition against *terefah* ("torn" flesh) connects back to v. 13 and forward to the full dietary code. The chapter that began with stolen livestock concludes with a call to sacred identity.

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Note

Exodus 22 continues the Book of the Covenant (*Sefer ha-Berit*) that began at 21:1 and will conclude at 23:33. The chapter exhibits a discernible structural progression from property laws (vv. 1-15) through personal-sexual law (vv. 16-17) to capital religious offenses (vv. 18-20) and finally to social-ethical commands with theological grounding (vv. 21-31). This movement -- from property to persons to God -- mirrors the theological logic of the Sinai legislation: material concerns are not dismissed but are progressively situated within a framework of covenant relationship and divine holiness.

The property-law section (vv. 1-15) is fully developed casuistic legislation addressing theft (vv. 1-4), agricultural damage (vv. 5-6), deposit and bailment (vv. 7-13), and borrowed property (vv. 14-15). The restitution scales are calibrated: five-fold for an ox (a capital asset and means of production), four-fold for a sheep (a lesser capital asset), and two-fold for goods found still in the thief's possession.

The transitional section (vv. 16-20) moves from casuistic ("if/when") to apodictic ("you shall not") form. The seduction law (vv. 16-17) is the last casuistic case; the sorcery prohibition (v. 18), the bestiality law (v. 19), and the idolatry law (v. 20) are all apodictic -- absolute prohibitions without conditional framing.

The final section (vv. 21-31) is entirely apodictic and increasingly theological. The laws protecting sojourners, widows, and orphans (vv. 21-24) are grounded in Israel's historical experience and in the divine character. The lending laws (vv. 25-27) are enforced by God himself. The concluding commands about reverence for God (v. 28), prompt offering of firstfruits (v. 29), consecration of the firstborn (v. 30), and dietary holiness (v. 31) bring the chapter to its theological climax: "men of holiness you shall be to me."

### Key Decisions

**"Pay" for *yeshallem* (throughout):** The piel of *shalam* ("to make whole, restore") appears over a dozen times in this chapter. LOGOS's "make restitution" captures the restorative force but becomes unwieldy with repetition. EMET's "pay" is used consistently, with the understanding (noted in the footnotes) that *yeshallem* means restorative payment, not mere financial transaction.

**"Ha-Elohim" retained (vv. 8-9):** Consistent with the precedent in 21:6, the Hebrew term is left untranslated in the juridical oath context, preserving the deliberate ambiguity between God and judges. In v. 28, where the article is absent, the standard rendering "God" is used.

**"Gracious am I" for *khannun ani* (v. 27):** The Hebrew predicate-first word order is preserved. This divine self-disclosure -- the ground of the pledge law -- anticipates the fuller theophanic formula of 34:6 and should retain its emphasis on the attribute rather than the subject.

**"Men of holiness" for *anshei-qodesh* (v. 31):** The construct chain is preserved rather than flattened to "holy men." Holiness is the governing noun, and the people are defined by their belonging to it -- not the reverse.

**"Devoted to destruction" for *yokhoram* (v. 20):** The sacral-dedicatory dimension of the *kherem* is preserved. The offender is not merely executed but placed under the ban -- wholly given over to YHWH through annihilation, in the same category as the hostile nations of the conquest narratives.

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 22 (pp. 64-65) preserves several notable divergences from the MT: (1) The one-verse offset in numbering (MT 22:1 = LXX 21:37) persists throughout. (2) In v. 3, the LXX adds *antapothaneitai* ("he shall die in return"), making the daytime killing of a thief a capital offense -- stronger than the MT's "there is bloodguilt for him." (3) In vv. 8-9, *ha-Elohim* is rendered *enopion tou theou* ("before God"), maintaining the divine rather than judicial interpretation. (4) In v. 18, the feminine singular *mekhashefah* ("sorceress") is rendered as masculine plural *pharmakous* ("sorcerers/poisoners"), broadening the gender scope. (5) In v. 20, *yokhoram* ("shall be devoted to destruction") becomes *olethreuthēsetai* ("shall be utterly destroyed"), losing the sacral-dedicatory nuance. (6) In v. 21, *ger* ("sojourner") is rendered *prosēluton* ("proselyte"), reflecting the Hellenistic shift from ethnic-social outsider to religious convert. (7) In v. 25, *neshekh* ("interest, bite") is rendered *ep' toko* ("at interest"), using the standard Greek financial term and losing the Hebrew's predatory metaphor. (8) In v. 28, *Elohim* is rendered as the plural *theous* ("gods"), which may reflect a reading of *Elohim* as referring to judges or dignitaries. (9) In v. 29, the compressed *mele'atekha ve-dim'akha* ("your fullness and your outflow") is rendered *aparchas alonos kai lenou* ("firstfruits of threshing floor and wine-vat"), making the metonymic referents explicit. (10) In v. 31, *anshei-qodesh* ("men of holiness") is rendered *andres hagioi* ("holy men"), reversing the construct-chain structure into a simple adjective-noun phrase.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
